content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Keith F. Collins 
Jones & Mayer 
3777 N. Harbor Blvd 
Fullerton, CA 92835 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-196 
Dear Mr. Collins: 
October 4, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Whittier City Council member 
Cathy Warner regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn 
Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we arc only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Warner make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office and near real property she 
owns and rents out to tenants? 
CONCLUSION 
Councilmember Warner may make, participate in making, and influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office or near real property she owns 
and rents out to tenants since these decisions will not financially affect her real property or business. 
FACTS 
Your office serves as legal counsel to the City of Whittier. Under a local ordinance, the City 
Council can designate certain buildings in the City as historic landmarks. The designation requires 
those buildings to be maintained in a manner that preserves their historical appearance. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-196 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember Warner and her husband rent a dental office space at 9209 Colima Road in 
Whittier where her husband operates a dental practice. Originally, the Wamers had a leasehold 
interest in the dental office, however the lease has expired over three years ago, and they are now 
renting the space on a month to month arrangement. This property is within 500 feet of 9200 
Colima Road, a medical office building being considered for landmark designation. 
Additionally, Councilmember Warner owns residential rental property at 14702 Christine 
Drive in Whittier. This property is within 500 feet of 14618 Whittier Boulevard where there exists a 
building which is also being considered for landmark designation. The Whittier Boulevard building 
contains a retail business that sells floral arrangements. A view in Google Earth shows that the 
Councilmember's property is buffered from the Whittier Boulevard building by two rows of homes, 
several buildings, and in part by Toyota of Whittier. 
Your office does not believe that it is reasonably foreseeable that a council vote to designate 
either property as a historical landmark would have a material financial effect on the council 
member's business and/or property interest. You stated that the City hired an experienced 
consultant to assist with putting together the list of buildings for potential historical landmark 
designation. The consultant informed your office that in all her years in this field she has never seen 
any data that indicates designating a building as a historical landmark has any effect, positive or 
negative, on surrounding property values. For this reason, your office feels that it is not reasonably 
foreseeable that the council's decision to designate certain buildings as historical landmarks will 
have a financial effect on their residential property and/or business interests, and if there was, such 
an effect it would not be material. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87 I 03 and 
include the following: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). An indirect 
interest in business or real property means a business or interest owned by the spouse or 
dependent child of the official. 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87 I 03(c).) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).) 


File No. A-16-196 
Page No. 3 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.2 (Section 87103.) 
Interests related to the Dental Office: The dental practice of the Councilmember's spouse is 
both a business interest under Sections 87103(a) and (d) and a source of income under Section 
87103(c). Both the dental practice and any clients of the business are considered sources of 
income.3 (Section 87103(c).) 
However, the Councilmember does not have a real property interest in the dental office. 
Section 82033 defines "an interest in real property" to include a leasehold interest. However, 
Regulation 18233 provides that a periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from the Act's 
definition of "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest." Because the Councilmember rents 
the office on a month-to-month basis, she does not have a financial interest in the property that 
could give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act. 
Interests related to the Rental Property: The Councilmember docs have an ownership 
interest in real property that she rents to a tenant. By virtue of the rental agreement she also has an 
interest in the tenant as a source of income:' (Section 87103(c).) Her rental business is also 
considered to be a business interest under Sections 87103(a) and (d). 
Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. (Regulation 1870 I.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, a financial 
effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. None of the Councilmember's interests related to her 
dental practice or her rental property are explicitly involved in the decision. 
Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, such as here, the 
foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation l 8701(b}.) 
? However, Regulation 18702.5 which provides that materiality standard for a disqualifying financial effect on 
an official's personal finances provides that if the governmental decision also affects a business entity or real property 
in which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and materiality is determined 
under Regulation 18702. l (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.5( c).) Thus, we do not 
further analyze possible effects on personal finances, 
1 We have not analyzed potential conflicts of interest related to clients who are sources of income because you 
have not provided facts pertaining to any clients. 
f We have not analyzed potential conflicts ofinterest related to the Councilmember's tenant because you have 
not provided facts pertaining to the tenant. 


Maleriality 
File No. A-16-196 
Page No. 4 
Business Interests: Regulation 18702. l (b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for a 
business interest.5 Regulation 18702. l (b) states in pertinent part as follows: 
"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the 
financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would 
find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in ... the value of a privately-held business entity." 
Nothing in your facts suggest that the decisions to designate properties near the dental office 
or rental properties as historical landmarks will contribute to a change in the value of her privately­
held business. 
Real Property Interest in Rental Property: A financial effect on real property is material if it 
meets any of the standards in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l) through (12). The standards relevant to the 
decision in question are: 
''( I 0) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
''( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Based on the distance from the site and number of intervening buildings, your facts do not 
suggest any changes to the neighborhood in which the Councilmember's property is located will 
result from the designation. Nor are there any facts suggesting that the decisions will affect the 
.1- Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's 
interest in a source of income. Regulation l 8702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, 
materiality is detennined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a 
business entity contained in Regulation 18 702.1. 


File No. A-16-196 
Page No. 5 
value of the Councilmember's property. Consequently, based on your facts, the Councilmember 
would not have a conflict of interest in the decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
. WaJlace 
t General Counsel, 
ivision

May Councilmember Warner make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office and near real property she 
owns and rents out to tenants?

Councilmember Warner may make, participate in making, and influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office or near real property she owns 
and rents out to tenants since these decisions will not financially affect her real property or business.

his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87 I 03 and

An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of

trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). An indirect 
interest in business or real property means a business or interest owned by the spouse or 
dependent child of the official. 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87 I 03(c).) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)  
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.2 (Section 87103.) 
Interests related to the Dental Office: The dental practice of the Councilmember's spouse is 
both a business interest under Sections 87103(a) and (d) and a source of income under Section

However, the Councilmember does not have a real property interest in the dental office.

Interests related to the Rental Property: The Councilmember docs have an ownership 
interest in real property that she rents to a tenant. By virtue of the rental agreement she also has an 
interest in the tenant as a source of income:' (Section 87103(c).) Her rental business is also 
considered to be a business interest under Sections 87103(a) and (d).

? However, Regulation 18702.5 which provides that materiality standard for a disqualifying financial effect on

in which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and materiality is determined 
under Regulation 18702. l (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.5( c).) Thus, we do not 
further analyze possible effects on personal finances, 
1 We have not analyzed potential conflicts of interest related to clients who are sources of income because you 
have not provided facts pertaining to any clients. 
f We have not analyzed potential conflicts ofinterest related to the Councilmember's tenant because you have 
not provided facts pertaining to the tenant. 


Maleriality  
Business Interests: Regulation 18702. l (b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for a 
business interest.5 Regulation 18702. l (b) states in pertinent part as follows: 
"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the 
financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would 
find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in ... the value of a privately-held business entity." 
Nothing in your facts suggest that the decisions to designate properties near the dental office

held business. 
Real Property Interest in Rental Property: A financial effect on real property is material if it 
meets any of the standards in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l) through (12). The standards relevant to the 
decision in question are: 
''( I 0) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
''( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Based on the distance from the site and number of intervening buildings, your facts do not 
suggest any changes to the neighborhood in which the Councilmember's property is located will 
result from the designation. Nor are there any facts suggesting that the decisions will affect the

value of the Councilmember's property. Consequently, based on your facts, the Councilmember

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Warner make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office and near real property she 
owns and rents out to tenants?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Warner may make, participate in making, and influence decisions regarding 
the historic landmark designation of buildings near her dental office or near real property she owns 
and rents out to tenants since these decisions will not financially affect her real property or business.
